Based on my search results, here's what I found about `@vitejs/plugin-react` and React 19 compatibility in 2025:

## Version Information

The latest version of `@vitejs/plugin-react` is 5.1.2, published about a month ago.

## React 19 Compatibility

Vite is the preferred tool for React 19 projects in 2025. This guide walks through setting up React 19 using Vite — a fast and efficient tool that makes the development process a breeze.

## Key Updates for React 19 Support

From the changelog, there are several important updates related to React 19:

The plugin now requires Node 20.19+ or 22.12+.(no content)React Compiler was updated to accept a target option and runtimeModule was removed. vite-plugin-react will still detect runtimeModule for backwards compatibility. When using a custom runtimeModule or target !== '19', the plugin will not try to pre-optimize react/compiler-runtime dependency. The react-compiler-runtime is now available on npm and can be used instead of the local shim for people using the compiler with React < 19.

## Using with React 18 (Backwards Compatibility)

Looks like we can have code compatible with both React 18 and 19.

## Alternative: SWC Plugin

Vite's React plugin enables key features like JSX support and React Fast Refresh. In 2025, using SWC (a Rust-based compiler) significantly speeds up development builds. Using `@vitejs/plugin-react-swc` replaces the traditional Babel transform, offering faster compilation speeds—ideal for large projects that don't require custom Babel plugins.

## Additional Features

Vite supports React Server Components (RSC) through the plugin. It utilizes the Environment API to provide low-level primitives that React frameworks can use to integrate RSC features.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Links: [{"title":"vite/packages/vite/CHANGELOG.md at main · vitejs/vite","url":"https://github.com/vitejs/vite/blob/main/packages/vite/CHANGELOG.md"},{"title":"Vite 6.0 is out! | Vite","url":"https://vite.dev/blog/announcing-vite6"},{"title":"Releases | Vite","url":"https://vite.dev/releases"},{"title":"Releases · vitejs/vite","url":"https://github.com/vitejs/vite/releases"},{"title":"vite - npm","url":"https://www.npmjs.com/package/vite?activeTab=versions"},{"title":"Vite 7.0 is out! | Vite","url":"https://vite.dev/blog/announcing-vite7"},{"title":"Migration from v6 | Vite","url":"https://vite.dev/guide/migration"},{"title":"Breaking Changes | Vite","url":"https://vite.dev/changes/"},{"title":"Change Log | Guide | Vite PWA","url":"https://vite-pwa-org.netlify.app/guide/change-log"},{"title":"CHANGELOG.md | React Router","url":"https://reactrouter.com/changelog"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Vite 6 and the latest versions:

## Vite 6 Release

Vite 6 is the most significant major release since Vite 2.(no content)The Vite team, contributors, and ecosystem partners announced the release of Vite 6.0.

### Key Features and Changes in Vite 6:
- Vite 6 added new capabilities with the new experimental Environment API.
- Vite adoption has been growing significantly, with npm downloads per week jumping from 7.5 million to 17 million since the release of Vite 5 a year ago.
- Notable changes include dropping Node 21 support, updating dotenv-expand to v12, HTML support for more asset sources, removing the fs.cachedChecks option, and proxy bypass with WebSocket.
- New frameworks have joined the Vite ecosystem, including TanStack Start, One, Ember, and others.

### Latest Version Information

The latest stable version on npm is 7.3.0, published 22 days ago.(no content)There is also a pre-release version v8.0.0-beta.5 available on GitHub releases.

### Vite 7 Has Been Released

Vite 7 has been released! It marks 5 years since Evan You sent the first commit to the Vite repo.(no content)Vite is now being downloaded 31 million times a week, with a 14 million increase in the last seven months since the previous major release.

Key changes in Vite 7:
- In Vite 7, the default browser target is changing from 'modules' to a new default: 'baseline-widely-available'. The set of browsers will be updated on each major to match the list of minimum browser versions compatible with Baseline Widely available features.
- Support for the Sass legacy API is removed. Vite now only supports the modern API.

### Resources
- Full changelog: https://github.com/vitejs/vite/blob/main/packages/vite/CHANGELOG.md
- Vite 6 announcement: https://vite.dev/blog/announcing-vite6
- Migration guide: https://vite.dev/guide/migration
- Breaking changes: https://vite.dev/changes/


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Here are the search results for Vite 6 official documentation:

## Vite 6 Documentation Overview

Vite 6 was the most significant major release since Vite 2, adding new capabilities with the new experimental Environment API.

### Official Documentation Resources

The official Vite documentation is available at **vite.dev**:

- **Getting Started Guide**: [vite.dev/guide/](https://vite.dev/guide/)
- **Releases**: [vite.dev/releases](https://vite.dev/releases)
- **Vite 6 Announcement**: [vite.dev/blog/announcing-vite6](https://vite.dev/blog/announcing-vite6)

### Key Features of Vite 6

Vite is getting more flexible with the new Environment API. These new APIs will allow framework authors to offer a dev experience closer to production and for the Ecosystem to share new building blocks.(no content)Vite 6 supports Node.js 18, 20, and 22+, similar to Vite 5. Node.js 21 support has been dropped.(no content)Vite drops Node.js support for older versions after their EOL. The Node.js 18 EOL is at the end of April 2025, after which we may release a new major to bump the required Node.js version.

### Current Version Status (2025-2026)

The latest version is 7.3.0, last published 22 days ago.(no content)Currently, regular patches are released for vite@7.2. Important fixes and security patches are backported to vite@6.4 and vite@7.1.

### Vite 7 Update

Vite now requires Node.js 20.19+, 22.12+. They have dropped Node.js 18, now that it has reached its EOL at the end of April 2025.

### LLM-Optimized Documentation

Vite provides optimized Markdown documentation at /llms.txt, or /llms-full.txt for the full documentation bundle.

Links: [{"title":"@vitejs/plugin-react - npm","url":"https://www.npmjs.com/package/@vitejs/plugin-react"},{"title":"React 19 Setup with Vite: Fast Setup in Minutes - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/a1guy/setting-up-your-react-19-development-environment-with-vite-4b7k"},{"title":"Getting Started | Vite","url":"https://vite.dev/guide/"},{"title":"vite-plugin-react/packages/plugin-react/CHANGELOG.md at main · vitejs/vite-plugin-react","url":"https://github.com/vitejs/vite-plugin-react/blob/main/packages/plugin-react/CHANGELOG.md"},{"title":"Set up React 19 Compiler in a Vite Typescript Project » All Mad Designs Blog","url":"https://allmaddesigns.com/set-up-react-19-compiler-in-a-vite-typescript-project/"},{"title":"Mastering Server-Side Rendering (SSR) in React 19 with Vite: The Ultimate Guide for Developers - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/yugjadvani/mastering-server-side-rendering-ssr-in-react-19-with-vite-the-ultimate-guide-for-developers-4mgm"},{"title":"Getting Started with React 19 and Vite - Cybox Solutions","url":"https://cyboxs.com/getting-started-with-react-19-and-vite/"},{"title":"@vitejs/plugin-react-oxc - npm","url":"https://www.npmjs.com/package/@vitejs/plugin-react-oxc"},{"title":"Releases · vitejs/vite-plugin-react","url":"https://github.com/vitejs/vite-plugin-react/releases"},{"title":"GitHub - vitejs/vite-plugin-react: The all-in-one Vite plugin for React projects.","url":"https://github.com/vitejs/vite-plugin-react"}]

Here's the information on setting up Vite 6 with React 19 using `@vitejs/plugin-react`:

## Basic Configuration

The `@vitejs/plugin-react` is the default Vite plugin for React projects.(no content)It enables Fast Refresh in development (requires react >= 16.9), uses the automatic JSX runtime, allows custom Babel plugins/presets, and has a small installation size.

### Basic `vite.config.js` Setup

The basic configuration includes importing defineConfig from 'vite' and react from '@vitejs/plugin-react', then exporting a config with the react plugin:

```javascript
import { defineConfig } from 'vite'
import react from '@vitejs/plugin-react'

export default defineConfig({
  plugins: [react()],
})
```

This includes .js, .jsx, .ts & .tsx files and excludes /node_modules/ by default.

## Vite 6 Requirements

Vite requires Node.js version 20.19+, 22.12+.(no content)The @vitejs/plugin-react also requires Node 20.19+ or 22.12+.(no content)Vite 6 is highly backward compatible, so setup remains straightforward.

## Creating a New Project

You can create a new Vite + React project by running: `npm create vite@latest my-react-app --template react`(no content)The supported templates include: vanilla, vanilla-ts, vue, vue-ts, react, react-ts, react-swc, react-swc-ts, preact, preact-ts, lit, lit-ts, svelte, svelte-ts, solid, solid-ts.

## Advanced Configuration with Path Aliases

You can add path aliases and other configurations:

```javascript
import { defineConfig } from 'vite';
import react from '@vitejs/plugin-react';
import path from 'path';

export default defineConfig({
  plugins: [react()],
  resolve: {
    alias: {
      '@': path.resolve(__dirname, './src'),
    },
  },
});
```

## React Compiler Support (for React 19)

When using a custom runtimeModule or target !== '19', the plugin will not try to pre-optimize react/compiler-runtime dependency. The react-compiler-runtime is now available on npm and can be used instead of the local shim for people using the compiler with React < 19.

For React Compiler with React 18:
You can configure the compiler with React 18 by installing `babel-plugin-react-compiler`, `react-compiler-runtime`, and `@babel/plugin-transform-react-jsx-development`.

## Key Features

When you run `npm run dev`, Vite starts a local web server, serves your source files directly as ES Modules, and applies transformations (JSX → JS, CSS Modules, etc.) on-demand as you import them. Since Vite doesn't bundle the entire app at once, startup is lightning-fast.(no content)When you run `npm run build`, Vite uses Rollup to bundle and optimize your code, minifies JavaScript and CSS to reduce the file size, and generates hashed filenames to enable long-term caching.

## Note on @vitejs/plugin-react-oxc

The @vitejs/plugin-react-oxc package is deprecated. You should use @vitejs/plugin-react instead, which automatically enables Oxc-based Fast Refresh transform on rolldown-vite.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.content:
# Vite Information Summary

**Latest Version:** v7.2.7

**Requirements:** Node.js version 20.19+, 22.12+, though some templates may require higher versions.

**React + TypeScript Setup:** The page lists "react-ts" as a supported template preset. You can scaffold a project using:
```
npm create vite@latest my-app -- --template react-ts
```

**Build Optimization:** While the page doesn't detail specific optimization options, it mentions that Vite uses Rollup for bundling and is "pre-configured to output highly optimized static assets for production." The documentation notes that "Vite by default targets Baseline Widely Available browsers" (released 2.5+ years ago). For detailed build configuration, users should consult the dedicated Build Options documentation.

**Additional Setup Options:** The documentation offers multiple ways to get started, including trying Vite online via StackBlitz without local installation, or scaffolding projects with various framework templates.

content.content:
# @vitejs/plugin-react CHANGELOG Summary

## Recent Changes

The most recent version is **5.1.2 (2025-12-08)**, which updates code to support newer `rolldown-vite`. Version 5.1.1 (2025-11-12) introduced a virtual module `@vitejs/plugin-react/preamble` for SSR HMR support and fixed raw Rolldown compatibility.

## React 19 Support

The changelog doesn't explicitly mention React 19 compatibility by version number. However, recent versions (5.x and 4.7.0+) support current React versions. For the most current React support, consult the project's README or package.json dependencies.

## React Compiler Configuration

The plugin offers several compiler-related options:

- **`babel.plugins`**: Add `'babel-plugin-react-compiler'` to enable the compiler
- **`compilationMode`**: Set to `"annotation"` to require explicit `"use memo"` directives
- **Environment filtering**: The compiler now skips processing on non-client environments (version 5.0.1+)

Key note: Older versions requiring a `runtimeModule` option are no longer supported as of version 5.0.0-beta.0. The newer compiler versions handle this automatically through the `target` option instead.

# @vitejs/plugin-react CHANGELOG Summary

## Recent Changes

The most recent version is **5.1.2 (2025-12-08)**, which updates code to support newer `rolldown-vite`. Version 5.1.1 (2025-11-12) introduced a virtual module `@vitejs/plugin-react/preamble` for SSR HMR support and fixed raw Rolldown compatibility.

## React 19 Support

The changelog doesn't explicitly mention React 19 compatibility by version number. However, recent versions (5.x and 4.7.0+) support current React versions. For the most current React support, consult the project's README or package.json dependencies.

## React Compiler Configuration

The plugin offers several compiler-related options:

- **`babel.plugins`**: Add `'babel-plugin-react-compiler'` to enable the compiler
- **`compilationMode`**: Set to `"annotation"` to require explicit `"use memo"` directives
- **Environment filtering**: The compiler now skips processing on non-client environments (version 5.0.1+)

Key note: Older versions requiring a `runtimeModule` option are no longer supported as of version 5.0.0-beta.0. The newer compiler versions handle this automatically through the `target` option instead.

input.prompt: What are the key features of Vite 6? What is the Environment API? What are the breaking changes? What Node.js versions are supported?

content.content:
# Vite 6: Key Information

## Key Features

Vite 6 is described as "the most significant major release since Vite 2." Main changes include:

- **Default `resolve.conditions`** values
- **JSON stringify** improvements
- **Extended HTML asset references** support
- **postcss-load-config** updates
- **Sass modern API** as default
- **Customizable CSS output filenames** in library mode

The release maintains backward compatibility for single-client SPAs and custom SSR applications.

## Environment API

The new Environment API makes Vite more adaptable for framework creators. As the announcement explains, "These new APIs will allow framework authors to offer a dev experience closer to production." The API is experimental and targets framework authors and plugin maintainers rather than typical end users. Detailed guidance is available in the Environment API Guides documentation.

## Breaking Changes

The announcement references a dedicated "Breaking Changes" page but doesn't enumerate all modifications in this post. Users should consult the detailed Migration Guide before upgrading.

## Node.js Support

**Vite 6 supports Node.js 18, 20, and 22+.** Version 21 support was discontinued. The project drops support for older versions following their end-of-life dates, with Node.js 18's EOL scheduled for April 2025.

Links: [{"title":"Configuring Vite | Vite","url":"https://vite.dev/config/"},{"title":"Features | Vite","url":"https://vite.dev/guide/features"},{"title":"Vite with TypeScript","url":"https://www.robinwieruch.de/vite-typescript/"},{"title":"Best Practices for React.js with Vite and TypeScript: What I Use and Why | by Terance Edmonds | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@taedmonds/best-practices-for-react-js-with-vite-and-typescript-what-i-use-and-why-f4482558ed89"},{"title":"Complete Guide to Setting Up React with TypeScript and Vite (2026) | by Robin Viktorsson | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@robinviktorsson/complete-guide-to-setting-up-react-with-typescript-and-vite-2025-468f6556aaf2"},{"title":"Understand configuring TypeScript in Vite","url":"https://app.studyraid.com/en/read/11791/374115/configuring-typescript-in-vite"},{"title":"A Comprehensive Guide to TypeScript Vite Config","url":"https://www.webdevtutor.net/blog/typescript-vite-config"},{"title":"Why does Vite create multiple TypeScript config files: tsconfig.json, tsconfig.app.json and tsconfig.node.json? - GeeksforGeeks","url":"https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/typescript/why-does-vite-create-multiple-typescript-config-files-tsconfigjson-tsconfigappjson-and-tsconfignodejson/"},{"title":"vite-tsconfig-paths - npm","url":"https://www.npmjs.com/package/vite-tsconfig-paths"},{"title":"How to add typescript to an existing vite react app? · vitejs/vite · Discussion #6799","url":"https://github.com/vitejs/vite/discussions/6799"}]

Here are the best practices for configuring TypeScript with Vite 6 in 2025:

## Key tsconfig.json Settings

### Required Compiler Options

You must set `"isolatedModules": true` in your tsconfig.json under compilerOptions, so that TypeScript will warn you against features that do not work with isolated transpilation.(no content)This is because esbuild only performs transpilation without type information and doesn't support certain features like const enum and implicit type-only imports.(no content)It is recommended to set `target` to ESNext or ES2022 or newer, or set `useDefineForClassFields` to `true` explicitly when configuring tsconfig.json.(no content)Vite starter templates have `"skipLibCheck": "true"` by default to avoid typechecking dependencies, as they may choose to only support specific versions and configurations of TypeScript.

### Multiple Config Files Structure

In Vite TypeScript projects you may have noticed three TypeScript configuration files: tsconfig.json, tsconfig.app.json, and tsconfig.node.json. Each file is used for a different purpose.(no content)The tsconfig.node.json is for TypeScript files that don't run in the browser, like your Vite config. These files run in a Node.js-like environment but still use modern JavaScript features.(no content)Multiple tsconfig files ensure proper type checking for different environments (app vs. Node).

### Client-Side Types

To shim the environment of client-side code in a Vite application, you can add `vite/client` to compilerOptions.types inside tsconfig.json.

## Path Aliases with vite-tsconfig-paths

The vite-tsconfig-paths plugin gives Vite the ability to resolve imports using TypeScript's path mapping.(no content)Use the vite-tsconfig-paths plugin to resolve the TypeScript alias path in vite.config.ts. Configure it like this:

```typescript
import { defineConfig } from 'vite';
import react from '@vitejs/plugin-react';
import tsconfigPaths from 'vite-tsconfig-paths';

export default defineConfig({
  plugins: [react(), tsconfigPaths()]
});
```

New in v6 of vite-tsconfig-paths: On-demand tsconfig discovery with `projectDiscovery: "lazy"`, automatic tsconfig reloads (including vite build --watch), and support for "absolute path" aliases.

## Vite Config Best Practices

When you create a new React project using Vite, the default vite.config.ts file is surprisingly minimal. This simplicity is intentional—Vite is designed to work out of the box with smart defaults, especially for common setups like React with TypeScript.(no content)Vite supports TypeScript config files. You can use vite.config.ts with the `defineConfig` helper function or with the `satisfies` operator.(no content)Vite uses esbuild to transpile TypeScript into JavaScript which is about 20-30x faster than vanilla tsc, and HMR updates can reflect in the browser in under 50ms.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Here are the best practices for configuring TypeScript with Vite 6 in 2025:

## Key tsconfig.json Settings

### Required Compiler Options

You must set `"isolatedModules": true` in your tsconfig.json under compilerOptions, so that TypeScript will warn you against features that do not work with isolated transpilation.(no content)This is because esbuild only performs transpilation without type information and doesn't support certain features like const enum and implicit type-only imports.(no content)It is recommended to set `target` to ESNext or ES2022 or newer, or set `useDefineForClassFields` to `true` explicitly when configuring tsconfig.json.(no content)Vite starter templates have `"skipLibCheck": "true"` by default to avoid typechecking dependencies, as they may choose to only support specific versions and configurations of TypeScript.

Each file is used for a different purpose.(no content)The tsconfig.node.json is for TypeScript files that don't run in the browser, like your Vite config. These files run in a Node.js-like environment but still use modern JavaScript features.(no content)Multiple tsconfig files ensure proper type checking for different environments (app vs. Node).

To shim the environment of client-side code in a Vite application, you can add `vite/client` to compilerOptions.types inside tsconfig.json.

content.content:
Web search results for query: "Vite large application performance optimization build chunking code splitting"

Links: [{"title":"How to optimize large projects with dynamic imports and code splitting in ViteJS? · vitejs/vite · Discussion #17730","url":"https://github.com/vitejs/vite/discussions/17730"},{"title":"Optimizing Your React Vite Application: A Guide to Reducing Bundle Size | by Shehzad Ahmed | Medium","url":"https://shaxadd.medium.com/optimizing-your-react-vite-application-a-guide-to-reducing-bundle-size-6b7e93891c96"},{"title":"Some chunks are larger than 500 KiB after minification · vitejs/vite · Discussion #9440","url":"https://github.com/vitejs/vite/discussions/9440"},{"title":"Taming 'Large Chunks' in Vite + React: Why It Happens and How I Shrunk My Main Bundle - Mykola Aleksandrov","url":"https://www.mykolaaleksandrov.dev/posts/2025/11/taming-large-chunks-vite-react/"},{"title":"Code Splitting in React w/ Vite. If you’ve a large application and… | by AkashSDas | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@akashsdas_dev/code-splitting-in-react-w-vite-eae8a9c39f6e"},{"title":"Vite code splitting that just works | Sambit Sahoo","url":"https://sambitsahoo.com/blog/vite-code-splitting-that-works.html"},{"title":"Mastering Vite's Chunking Strategy: Best Practices and Tips","url":"https://runebook.dev/en/articles/vite/guide/build/chunking-strategy"},{"title":"Optimize Vite Build Time: A Comprehensive Guide - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/perisicnikola37/optimize-vite-build-time-a-comprehensive-guide-4c99"},{"title":"Why Vite | Vite","url":"https://vite.dev/guide/why"},{"title":"Splitting vendor chunk with Vite and loading them async - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/tassiofront/splitting-vendor-chunk-with-vite-and-loading-them-async-15o3"}]

Here's what I found about Vite large application performance optimization with build chunking and code splitting:

## Key Optimization Strategies

### 1. Dynamic Imports and Code Splitting
"Optimizing large projects with dynamic imports and code splitting in ViteJS can significantly improve performance." Dynamic imports allow you to load modules only when they are needed, which reduces the initial bundle size.(no content)If you have a large application and you're trying to keep the performance budget under 100kb for the initial page load, then you would probably need to do code splitting. Code splitting is a performance optimization technique.

### 2. Manual Chunks Configuration
You can manually control code splitting using Rollup's `manualChunks` option in your Vite config to group specific modules into custom chunks (e.g., vendor libraries like React, UI frameworks).(no content)You can import dependencies from package.json and modify the rollup output chunks to split each dependency from the vendor bundle. When Rollup builds your app, it will split the vendor chunk based on the `output.manualChunks` configuration.

### 3. Route-Level Lazy Loading
If your application features multiple pages, consider using React Router to split routes into separate chunks. By dynamically loading routes, you ensure that users only download the code they need. This can lead to lightning-fast navigation and a smoother user experience.(no content)Use route-level lazy loading with React.lazy + Suspense, and feature-level lazy imports for rarely used heavy widgets.

### 4. Handling Large Chunk Warnings
If you've seen Vite's warning about "Some chunks are larger than 500 kB after minification", this is common. You can address it by using manualChunks, React.lazy, and route-level code splitting to shrink your main bundle.(no content)Vite recommends three approaches: using dynamic import() to code-split the application, using `build.rollupOptions.output.manualChunks` to improve chunking, or adjusting the chunk size limit via `build.chunkSizeWarningLimit`.

### 5. Vendor Chunk Splitting
Vite removed automatic vendor splitting since version 2.9 - now, by default, the index also has the production dependencies code. However, Vite provides a plugin: `splitVendorChunkPlugin` that you can import from 'vite' to split vendor chunks.(no content)Note that `splitVendorChunkPlugin` is deprecated in Vite v5 - `manualChunks` is now sufficient.

### 6. Bundle Analysis
Use the rollup-plugin-visualizer to analyze your bundle and remove accidental bloat.

### 7. Additional Best Practices
Cherry-pick imports and prefer ESM builds. Swap heavy dependencies (like Moment → Day.js), reduce icon sets, and reconsider giant UI kits. Use prefetch/preload for predictable navigation. Keep CSS lean as large CSS can also delay rendering.(no content)For optimal loading performance in production, it's still better to bundle your code with tree-shaking, lazy-loading and common chunk splitting for better caching.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

However, Vite provides a plugin: `splitVendorChunkPlugin` that you can import from 'vite' to split vendor chunks.(no content)Note that `splitVendorChunkPlugin` is deprecated in Vite v5 - `manualChunks` is now sufficient.

input.prompt: What are all the main configuration options in Vite? What are the build options? What are the server options? What optimization options are available?

input.query: Vite 6 build.rollupOptions manualChunks configuration example React

content.content:
# Vite Configuration Options

Based on the documentation page, Vite's main configuration categories are:

## Primary Configuration Sections

The page references several key configuration areas accessible through the sidebar navigation:

1. **Shared Options** - Common settings applicable across environments
2. **Server Options** - Development server configuration
3. **Build Options** - Production build settings
4. **Preview Options** - Settings for previewing built applications
5. **Dep Optimization Options** - Dependency pre-bundling configuration
6. **SSR Options** - Server-side rendering specific settings
7. **Worker Options** - Web worker configuration

## Key Configuration Features

The documentation notes that Vite supports:

- **Conditional configuration**: "If the config needs to conditionally determine options based on the command (`serve` or `build`), the mode being used, if it's an SSR build (`isSsrBuild`), or is previewing the build (`isPreview`), it can export a function instead"

- **Async configuration**: The ability to call async functions within configuration exports

- **Environment variables**: Using the `loadEnv` helper to manually load environment variables that influence configuration itself

The page indicates these sections contain detailed specifications, though the full details of each category aren't expanded on this particular documentation index page.

input.prompt: What are all the build options available in Vite? What is target, minify, sourcemap, rollupOptions? What are the optimization options?

content.content:
# Vite Build Options Overview

## Key Build Options

**build.target**: Specifies browser compatibility. The default `'baseline-widely-available'` targets Chrome 107+, Edge 107+, Firefox 104+, and Safari 16+. You can also use `'esnext'` for minimal transpiling or specify custom targets like `'es2015'` or `'chrome58'`.

**build.minify**: Controls code minification. Options include `false` (disabled), `'esbuild'` (default, 20-40x faster than terser), or `'terser'`. The documentation notes that minification in lib mode with ES format doesn't remove whitespace to preserve tree-shaking.

**build.sourcemap**: Determines source map generation. Set to `false` (default), `true` for separate files, `'inline'` for data URIs, or `'hidden'` to suppress comments in bundled files.

**build.rollupOptions**: Allows direct customization of the underlying Rollup bundler configuration, merged with Vite's internal settings.

## Additional Essential Options

- **build.outDir**: Output directory (default: `dist`)
- **build.assetsDir**: Asset subfolder (default: `assets`)
- **build.emptyOutDir**: Auto-clear output before build (default: `true`)
- **build.cssCodeSplit**: Enable/disable CSS splitting (default: `true`)
- **build.lib**: Configure library mode with entry points and output formats

The documentation also covers SSR builds, manifest generation, and module preloading polyfills for comprehensive production optimization.

toolUseResult.result:
# Vite Build Options Overview

## Key Build Options

**build.target**: Specifies browser compatibility. The default `'baseline-widely-available'` targets Chrome 107+, Edge 107+, Firefox 104+, and Safari 16+. You can also use `'esnext'` for minimal transpiling or specify custom targets like `'es2015'` or `'chrome58'`.

**build.minify**: Controls code minification. Options include `false` (disabled), `'esbuild'` (default, 20-40x faster than terser), or `'terser'`. The documentation notes that minification in lib mode with ES format doesn't remove whitespace to preserve tree-shaking.

**build.sourcemap**: Determines source map generation. Set to `false` (default), `true` for separate files, `'inline'` for data URIs, or `'hidden'` to suppress comments in bundled files.

**build.rollupOptions**: Allows direct customization of the underlying Rollup bundler configuration, merged with Vite's internal settings.

## Additional Essential Options

- **build.outDir**: Output directory (default: `dist`)
- **build.assetsDir**: Asset subfolder (default: `assets`)
- **build.emptyOutDir**: Auto-clear output before build (default: `true`)
- **build.cssCodeSplit**: Enable/disable CSS splitting (default: `true`)
- **build.lib**: Configure library mode with entry points and output formats

The documentation also covers SSR builds, manifest generation, and module preloading polyfills for comprehensive production optimization.

content.title: [Typo]: The alternative way to configure react compiler with vitejs doesn't work · Issue #8148 · reactjs/react.dev
Mastering React 19’s Compiler: A Complete Guide to Modern React Development | by Noor Mohamad | Oct, 2025 | Medium

content.text: Now I have gathered comprehensive information. Let me write the research document:

I have completed the research and created the documentation file. Here is a summary of what was found:

## Research Summary

### Version Information (January 2026)

**Current Stable Versions:**
- **Vite**: 7.3.0 (latest), with Vite 6.4.x still receiving LTS security patches
- **@vitejs/plugin-react**: 5.1.2 (December 2025)
- **React Compiler**: v1.0.x (released October 2025)

**Node.js Requirements:**
- Vite 6: Node.js 18, 20, 22+ (Node 18 EOL April 2025)
- Vite 7: Node.js 20.19+, 22.12+ (Node 18 dropped)

### Key Findings

1. **React 19 Compatibility**: `@vitejs/plugin-react` 5.x fully supports React 19 with built-in React Compiler integration options.

2. **React Compiler**: Now stable at v1.0, can be enabled via Babel plugin configuration in `@vitejs/plugin-react`. Must be first in the Babel plugins list.

3. **TypeScript**: Critical settings include `isolatedModules: true` (required for esbuild), `moduleResolution: "bundler"`, and `types: ["vite/client"]`.

4. **Performance for Large Apps**:
   - Use `manualChunks` for vendor splitting (deprecated `splitVendorChunkPlugin`)
   - Route-level lazy loading with `React.lazy()` + `Suspense`
   - Bundle analysis with `rollup-plugin-visualizer`
   - `esbuild` minifier is 20-40x faster than terser

5. **Vite 6 Features**: Environment API (experimental) for better SSR/RSC support, Sass modern API as default.

The research document has been written to:
`/Users/anotonio.silva/antonio/mms-last-attempt/_memory/vite-research.md`
